memes.label: "Chapter 1: April 9 – 15"
memes.description: "Lord Eldon seems quite beaten. Lord Eldon went to Windsor to-day with petitions."

memes.label: "Chapter 10: August 13 – 31"
memes.description: "Drummond, whom I saw, said the Duke was delighted with the account of the Jaghirdars of the Kistna."

memes.description: "However, even admitting that the Russians would be content with the independence of Greece within the Morea (with…"

memes.label: "Chapter 12: September 26 – October 7"
memes.description: "Polignac seems excellently well disposed. He would act cordially with us if he dared."

memes.label: "Chapter 13: October 8 – 12"
memes.description: "We must recollect that Turkey had bound herself to acquiesce in the decision of the Conference upon the Greek…"

memes.label: "Chapter 14: October 13 – 29"
memes.description: "That the general treaty contains no guarantee is evident from the specific guarantee of the cessions made by Saxony to…"

memes.label: "Chapter 15: October 30 – November 13"
memes.description: "I think it is clear that the invasion of India could not be attempted till the third year; but when should we begin to…"

memes.label: "Chapter 16: November 16 – 21"
memes.description: "The Cabinet seemed generally to acquiesce in the expediency of only having a Committee this year."

memes.label: "Chapter 17: November 25 – December 15"
memes.description: "Told Bankes what the Duke wished respecting the Charter; but I likewise told him it had not yet been so determined in…"

memes.label: "Chapter 18: December 16 – 29"
memes.description: "There is a good paper of Aberdeen's to Sir R."

memes.label: "Chapter 19: December 31 – January 8"
memes.description: "I was glad to have my letter. I can defend every word in it."

memes.label: "Chapter 2: April 16 – 29"
memes.description: "Reference is to be made to Ireland to ascertain the feeling about it there. Bankes came at twelve o'clock."

memes.label: "Chapter 20: January 12 – 14"
memes.description: "I read Lord Stuart de Rothesay's last despatches and Lord Heytesbury's."

memes.label: "Chapter 21: January 15 – 27"
memes.description: "The Duke gave the Cabinet an account of his interview with the King."

memes.label: "Chapter 22: January 28 – February 6"
memes.description: "The 'Times' publishes my letter to Malcolm to-day, with comments."

memes.label: "Chapter 23: February 8 – 12"
memes.description: "In the meantime it appears that Mr. Robertson, who is at Mexico, remonstrated strongly with M."

memes.label: "Chapter 24: February 13 – 25"
memes.description: 'On the East Retford [Footnote: It will be remembered that this question had led to the resignation of Huskinsson and…'

memes.label: "Chapter 25: February 26 – March 13"
memes.description: "We divided well. There being but fifteen present for Lord Stanhope's motion, and ten proxies. Chairs at 11."

memes.label: "Chapter 26: March 17 – April 3"
memes.description: "In all this there seems to me unfairness. If the tax be imposed as it is proposed, it will be very difficult to include…"

memes.label: "Chapter 27: April 6 – 26"
memes.description: "Wrote to the Duke to tell him I had not yet received the Duke of Devonshire's memorandum respecting Sir W."

memes.label: "Chapter 28: April 27 – May 3"
memes.description: "Thus he has obtained almost all he asked, and what he most wanted, the money."

memes.label: "Chapter 29: May 4 – 11"
memes.description: "As to the last point there is an anachronism, as the orders not to leave Calcutta as a Government arrived after the…"

memes.label: "Chapter 3: April 30 – May 6"
memes.description: "I have always been for getting out of the treaty."

memes.label: "Chapter 30: May 12 – 21"
memes.description: "Read the memorandum on Hyderabad a second time, and sent it with the proposed letter and alterations to the Duke."

memes.label: "Chapter 31: May 23 – 28"
memes.description: "Rode to the Cabinet at three from Roehampton. The bulletin is that the King had had embarrassments in his breathing."

memes.label: "Chapter 32: May 29 – June 13"
memes.description: "The Commons passed the King's Signature Bill without a word."

memes.label: "Chapter 33: June 15 – 25"
memes.description: "It is like paying money in consequence of a threatening letter. If it is done once there is no stopping."

memes.label: "Chapter 34: June 26 – 28"
memes.description: "The King much weaker. At half-past eight this morning I received a Cabinet box containing the bulletin signed by…"

memes.label: "Chapter 35: June 29"
memes.description: "As the King passed through the line we formed for him to go to the window he came up to me and said he must begin by…"

memes.label: "Chapter 36: June 30 – July 2"
memes.description: "We had East Retford again. Lord Londonderry, whom Lord Durham puts forward as his tool, moved an adjournment."

memes.label: "Chapter 37: July 3 – 12"
memes.description: "Lord Bathurst says W. Horton is a gentleman. I doubted it. He rather wishes to have Wilmot in office."

memes.label: "Chapter 38: July 13 – 17"
memes.description: "All these difficulties I foresaw when the Lords adopted the Commons' resolution; but I suppose Canning forced it upon…"

memes.label: "Chapter 39: July 19 – 22"
memes.description: "We then came to the consideration of the East Retford question. All the press were for giving up the Bill."

memes.label: "Chapter 4: May 7 – 21"
memes.description: "There are disturbances at Manchester, which look rather serious. Nothing in the House."

memes.label: "Chapter 40: July 24 – August 2"
memes.description: "I said I did not entertain much fear of the French having Algiers."

memes.label: "Chapter 41: August 3 – 13"
memes.description: "The Guards are disorganised and desert. The Swiss only are said to remain with the King, who it is thought is gone to…"

memes.label: "Chapter 42: August 18 – 24"
memes.description: "The new King's oath-taking was flatly received. As long as he can keep La Fayette with him he is master of Paris."

memes.label: "Chapter 43: August 26 – September 5"
memes.description: "I suggested that the most important question was the revision of taxation."

memes.label: "Chapter 44: September 6 – 14"
memes.description: "The Emperor of Russia proposes to cut the Greek question short by proposing the crown at once, without the intervention…"

memes.label: "Chapter 45: September 20 – 29, 1830"
memes.description: 'Wrote a long letter to Hardinge upon the political consequences of Huskisson''s death, [Footnote: He was killed, as is…'

memes.label: "Chapter 46: September 30 – October 2"
memes.description: "The Consul at Ostend reports that the King's troops evacuated Brussels on Sunday night; that reinforcements from the…"

memes.label: "Chapter 47: October 3 – 9"
memes.description: "It would be inconvenient if the King should wish to go to Edinburgh next year. Charles X."

memes.label: "Chapter 48: October 11 – 16"
memes.description: "The farmers now leave their thrashing machines out in the fields to be broken."

memes.label: "Chapter 49: October 19 – 26"
memes.description: "He asked whether they were distinctly to understand that the Cabinet had decided upon the termination of the monopoly?"

memes.label: "Chapter 5: May 22 – June 10"
memes.description: "The King, our master, is the weakest man in England. He hates the Duke of Cumberland. He wishes his death."

memes.label: "Chapter 50: October 27 – November 1"
memes.description: "at two. Addresses from the Church of Scotland, and the Lord Mayor and Corporation of Dublin. Dr."

memes.label: "Chapter 51: November 3 – 6"
memes.description: "I had prepared a paragraph to be used in case it had been thought right to say anything about India."

memes.label: "Chapter 52: November 8 – 9"
memes.description: "Hardinge told me there was a proposal to Palmerston and others in the summer, and they at once started the difficulty…"

memes.label: "Chapter 53: November 10 – 14"
memes.description: "It was determined to endeavour to induce the mob to disperse as soon as the Rotunda was full, and then to read the Riot…"

memes.label: "Chapter 54: November 15 – 18"
memes.description: "It appears certain that at Carlisle the 9th was looked to as the day of signal to them and to all England."

memes.label: "Chapter 55"
memes.description: "I told him Peel would be in Opposition in a fortnight, as soon as he recovered his health and his spirits."

memes.label: "Chapter 6: June 11 – 24"
memes.description: "We had some conversation as to the charter. The Duke seems rather inclined to continue the name of the Company."

memes.label: "Chapter 7: June 25 – July 9"
memes.description: "At the office found a letter with enclosures from Colonel Macdonald, dated Tabriz April 20."

memes.label: "Chapter 8: July 10 – 22"
memes.description: "He told me Lieutenant-Colonel Skinner was a man of large landed property."

memes.description: "Chairs at eleven. Told them of the danger in which they were, from the feeling of the mercantile districts and of the…"

memes.label: "Front Matter"
memes.description: 'Title page and front matter of A Political Diary, 1828-1830, Volume II by Edward Law, 1st Earl of Ellenborough, edited by Lord Colchester.'

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Archbishop of Canterbury
memes.description: 'William Howley (1766–1848), Archbishop of Canterbury from 1828 to 1848, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary during debates on Catholic emancipation and ecclesiastical matters.'

memes.label: "Banking and Currency"
memes.description: "Issues of banking regulation, the Bank of England's charter, and currency policy during the late 1820s."

memes.label: Belgian Revolution
memes.description: 'The 1830 revolution in the United Kingdom of the Netherlands leading to Belgian independence, a major diplomatic crisis during the final months of Wellington''s ministry.'

memes.label: Bishop of Oxford
memes.description: 'Edward Legge (1767–1827), Bishop of Oxford from 1816 to 1827, described by Ellenborough as "a great Grecian" who died during the diary period, opening a significant ecclesiastical appointment.'

memes.label: British Cabinet
memes.description: 'The Cabinet of the Duke of Wellington''s government (1828–1830), the central decision-making body of the British executive, in which Ellenborough served and recorded its deliberations in his diary.'

memes.label: "British Foreign Policy"
memes.description: "The conduct of British diplomacy under the Duke of Wellington's ministry, 1828–1830, with Lord Aberdeen as Foreign Secretary."

memes.label: Captain Harvey
memes.description: 'Captain Harvey of the 4th Dragoons, a British officer who served as interpreter and intermediary between the British and Persian courts during the late 1820s.'

memes.label: "Catholic Emancipation"
memes.description: "The political movement and legislative process leading to the Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829, which removed most restrictions on Roman Catholics in the United Kingdom."

memes.label: Colonel Macdonald
memes.description: 'Colonel Macdonald, British envoy to Persia stationed at Tabriz, who reported on the Russo-Persian conflict of 1828.'

memes.label: Colonel Monteith
memes.description: 'Colonel Monteith, a British military officer who visited Algiers and reported on the French expedition there in 1830.'

memes.label: Colonel Salmond
memes.description: 'Colonel Salmond, a British military officer who appears in Ellenborough''s diary in connection with administrative procedures and official business.'

memes.label: Colonel Skinner
memes.description: 'Lieutenant-Colonel Skinner, a British officer in India with large landed property, described in the diary as a "feudal chieftain" who raised his corps from his own estates.'

memes.label: Colonel Tod
memes.description: 'Colonel James Tod (1782–1835), British officer and author of "Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan," whose writings Ellenborough read and admired.'

memes.label: Colonel Willoughby Cotton
memes.description: 'Colonel Willoughby Cotton, a British military officer who commanded in the Ava (Burma) campaign and served as Adjutant-General of the King''s troops in Bengal.'

memes.label: "Corn Laws"
memes.description: "The protective tariffs on imported grain that were a major source of political controversy in 1820s Britain."

memes.label: Count Guilleminot
memes.description: 'Count Armand Charles Guilleminot (1774–1840), French general and diplomat, ambassador to the Ottoman Empire during the Russo-Turkish War.'

memes.label: Don Miguel
memes.description: 'Dom Miguel I (1802–1866), King of Portugal, whose seizure of the throne from his niece Donna Maria created a major diplomatic crisis for the European powers.'

memes.label: Don Pedro
memes.description: 'Dom Pedro I (1798–1834), Emperor of Brazil and briefly King of Portugal, a central figure in the Portuguese succession crisis of the late 1820s.'

memes.label: Donna Maria
memes.description: 'Donna Maria II (1819–1853), Queen of Portugal, daughter of Emperor Don Pedro of Brazil, whose claim to the Portuguese throne was a subject of European diplomacy in the diary.'

memes.label: Duchess of Clarence
memes.description: 'Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen (1792–1849) — wife of William, Duke of Clarence (later William IV), who was considered for the Regency and appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a figure in court and succession politics.'

memes.label: Duchess of Cumberland
memes.description: 'Frederica of Mecklenburg-Strelitz (1778–1841) — wife of Ernest Augustus, Duke of Cumberland, noted in Ellenborough''s diary for her political influence and involvement in royal communications.'

memes.label: Duchess of Gloucester
memes.description: 'Princess Mary (1776–1857), Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh, fourth daughter of George III, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a figure at court during the final days of George IV.'

memes.label: Duchess of Kent
memes.description: 'Princess Victoria of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld (1786–1861) — mother of Queen Victoria, whose potential Regency and allowance were subjects of political discussion in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Duke of Bedford
memes.description: 'John Russell, 6th Duke of Bedford (1766–1839), a prominent Whig peer and supporter of parliamentary reform.'

memes.label: Duke of Buccleuch
memes.description: 'Walter Montagu Douglas Scott, 5th Duke of Buccleuch (1806–1884), Tory peer and landowner who appears in Ellenborough''s diary during his early political career.'

memes.label: Duke of Buckingham
memes.description: 'Richard Temple-Nugent-Brydges-Chandos-Grenville, 1st Duke of Buckingham and Chandos (1776–1839), Lord Steward of the Household and a Whig-leaning peer who frequently opposed the Wellington ministry in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Duke of Clarence
memes.description: 'Prince William Henry, Duke of Clarence (1765–1837), later King William IV, third son of George III and heir presumptive during the 1828–1830 period covered by Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Duke of Cumberland
memes.description: 'Ernest Augustus, Duke of Cumberland and Teviotdale (1771–1851), fifth son of George III, ultra-Tory peer and later King of Hanover.'

memes.label: Duke of Devonshire
memes.description: 'William Cavendish, 6th Duke of Devonshire (1790–1858), Whig grandee and landlord, mentioned in Ellenborough''s diary in connection with diplomatic and Irish appointments.'

memes.label: Duke of Gloucester
memes.description: 'Prince William Frederick, Duke of Gloucester and Edinburgh (1776–1834), member of the British royal family, great-grandson of George II, and Chancellor of the University of Cambridge.'

memes.label: Duke of Leinster
memes.description: 'Augustus Frederick FitzGerald, 3rd Duke of Leinster (1791–1874), premier peer of Ireland who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a Whig-leaning magnate ready to sign protest declarations.'

memes.label: Duke of Montrose
memes.description: 'James Graham, 3rd Duke of Montrose (1755–1836), a Scottish Tory peer who served in the Wellington administration and later resigned his office.'

memes.label: Duke of Newcastle
memes.description: 'Henry Pelham-Clinton, 4th Duke of Newcastle (1785–1851), a staunch Tory peer and opponent of Catholic emancipation.'

memes.label: Duke of Norfolk
memes.description: 'Bernard Edward Howard, 12th Duke of Norfolk (1765–1842), Earl Marshal of England and a prominent Roman Catholic peer, appearing in Ellenborough''s diary during the Catholic emancipation period.'

memes.label: Duke of Northumberland
memes.description: 'Hugh Percy, 3rd Duke of Northumberland (1785–1847), British peer and Tory politician who served as Lord-Lieutenant of Ireland from 1829 to 1830.'

memes.label: Duke of Orleans
memes.description: 'Louis Philippe III, Duke of Orleans (1773–1850), proclaimed King of the French in August 1830 following the July Revolution, a key figure in the diplomatic crisis that followed.'

memes.label: Duke of Richmond
memes.description: 'Charles Gordon-Lennox, 5th Duke of Richmond (1791–1860), a prominent Tory peer and frequent parliamentary antagonist of the Wellington ministry during 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Duke of Rutland
memes.description: 'John Manners, 5th Duke of Rutland (1778–1857), Tory peer and staunch opponent of Catholic emancipation, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a key vote in the Lords during the Catholic Relief Bill debates.'

memes.label: Duke of Sussex
memes.description: 'Prince Augustus Frederick, Duke of Sussex (1773–1843), sixth son of George III, a Whig-leaning royal whose political influence was noted with suspicion in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Duke of Wellington
memes.description: 'Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington (1769–1852), Prime Minister of the United Kingdom 1828–1830, central figure in Edward Law''s political diary.'

memes.label: Earl Bathurst
memes.description: 'Henry Bathurst, 3rd Earl Bathurst (1762–1834), served as Lord President of the Council in the Duke of Wellington''s Tory government (1828–1830), acting as a senior Cabinet confidant and frequent host of ministerial dinners recorded in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: East India Company
memes.description: 'The East India Company — the chartered corporation that governed British India — was the subject of intense Cabinet debate during the Wellington government, as recorded in Ellenborough''s diary, centering on the question of whether India should continue under Company rule or be transferred to direct Crown governance.'

memes.label: Sir Edward Knatchbull
memes.description: 'Sir Edward Knatchbull (1781–1849), a Tory MP and leader of a parliamentary faction opposed to Catholic emancipation.'

memes.label: Edward Stanley
memes.description: 'Edward Smith-Stanley (1799–1869), styled Mr. Stanley in 1828–1830 and later 14th Earl of Derby, future Prime Minister.'

memes.label: Emperor Nicholas
memes.description: 'Nicholas I (1796–1855), Emperor of Russia from 1825, whose foreign policy and moderate views were closely observed by the British government during 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Franchise Bill 1829
memes.description: 'Parliamentary legislation in 1829 to revise electoral franchises in East Retford and Galway, a significant legislative item in Wellington''s first parliamentary session.'

memes.label: General Muffling
memes.description: 'General Friedrich Karl Ferdinand von Müffling (1775–1851), Prussian general and military diplomat, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as an intermediary in the Russo-Turkish peace negotiations at Constantinople.'

memes.label: George Canning
memes.description: 'George Canning (1770–1827), British statesman and briefly Prime Minister in 1827, whose political legacy shaped the Canningite faction throughout the 1828–1830 period.'

memes.label: George IV
memes.description: 'George IV (1762–1830), King of the United Kingdom from 1820 to 1830, whose reign encompassed the Catholic emancipation crisis and the end of the Tory ascendancy chronicled in the diary.'

memes.label: Greek War of Independence
memes.description: 'The Greek War of Independence (1821–1830) and its diplomatic resolution — the "Greek Question" — dominated British foreign policy during the Wellington government, involving the Treaty of London, the Battle of Navarino, the [[Portuguese Civil War]], and the eventual establishment of an independent Greek state under Prince Leopold.'

memes.label: Henry Brougham
memes.description: 'Henry Brougham, 1st Baron Brougham and Vaux (1778–1868), Whig politician, lawyer, and reformer, a major figure in the House of Commons throughout the period of Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: 'The House of Commons — the elected lower house of the British Parliament — was the arena where the Wellington government''s legislative programme lived or died, and where the gathering forces of reform, recorded in Ellenborough''s diary, would ultimately bring the Tory ministry down in 1830.'

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: 'The House of Lords, the upper chamber of the British Parliament, where Ellenborough sat and recorded debates, divisions, and the passage of legislation including the Catholic Relief Bill and Franchise Bill.'

memes.label: "Irish Question"
memes.description: "The political and religious issues concerning Ireland in British politics during the late 1820s, including Catholic emancipation and the Act of Union."

memes.label: July Revolution 1830
memes.description: 'The July 1830 revolution in France that overthrew Charles X and installed Louis-Philippe, significantly altering the diplomatic landscape during Wellington''s final months in office.'

memes.label: Lord Aberdeen
memes.description: 'George Hamilton-Gordon, 4th Earl of Aberdeen (1784–1860), served as Foreign Secretary in the Duke of Wellington''s Tory government from 1828 to 1830, overseeing British diplomacy during a turbulent period of European revolutions and the Greek Question.'

memes.label: Lord Abinger
memes.description: 'James Scarlett, 1st Baron Abinger (1769–1844), English lawyer and Whig-turned-Tory politician, Attorney-General under Wellington, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary during the 1829 legal promotions.'

memes.label: Lord Althorp
memes.description: 'John Charles Spencer, Viscount Althorp (1782–1845), Whig politician, Chancellor of the Exchequer and Leader of the House of Commons under Lord Grey, instrumental in passing the Reform Bill.'

memes.label: Lord Amherst
memes.description: 'William Amherst, 1st Earl Amherst (1773–1857), British diplomat and Governor-General of India, formerly Ambassador to China.'

memes.label: Lord Anglesey
memes.description: 'Henry Paget, 1st Marquess of Anglesey (1768–1854), Lord Lieutenant of Ireland during the Wellington administration and a central figure in the Catholic emancipation controversy.'

memes.label: Lord Ashley
memes.description: 'Anthony Ashley-Cooper, Lord Ashley (1801–1885), later 7th Earl of Shaftesbury — Tory MP and junior minister during the Wellington administration, later renowned as a social reformer.'

memes.label: Lord Beresford
memes.description: "William Carr Beresford, 1st Viscount Beresford (1768–1854), British general and politician, Master-General of the Ordnance in Wellington's cabinet."

memes.label: Lord Camden
memes.description: 'John Jeffreys Pratt, 1st Marquess Camden (1759–1840), British peer and Tory politician who served as Lord Lieutenant of Ireland and appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a loyal supporter of Wellington''s government.'

memes.label: Lord Carlisle
memes.description: 'George Howard, 6th Earl of Carlisle (1773–1848) — Whig politician and cabinet minister, served as Chief Commissioner of Woods and Forests and later Lord Privy Seal.'

memes.label: Lord Cathcart
memes.description: 'William Cathcart, 1st Earl Cathcart (1755–1843), British general and diplomat, Colonel of the Horse Guards, noted in Ellenborough''s diary for his absence from ceremonial duties.'

memes.label: Lord Chandos
memes.description: 'Richard Temple-Nugent-Brydges-Chandos-Grenville, Marquess of Chandos (1797–1861), later 2nd Duke of Buckingham and Chandos, a Tory politician courted for Cabinet office in 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Lord Clanricarde
memes.description: 'Ulick de Burgh, 1st Marquess of Clanricarde (1802–1874) — Anglo-Irish Whig peer and politician, supporter of Catholic Emancipation, later Postmaster General.'

memes.label: Lord Clare
memes.description: 'John FitzGibbon, 2nd Earl of Clare (1792–1851), Irish peer and colonial administrator, later Governor of Bombay (1831–1835).'

memes.label: Lord Cleveland
memes.description: 'William Vane, 1st Duke of Cleveland (1766–1842), Whig peer and political supporter of Lord Grey, a faithful government supporter during the Wellington administration despite his Whig affiliations.'

memes.label: Lord Combermere
memes.description: 'Stapleton Cotton, 1st Viscount Combermere (1773–1865), British army officer who served as Commander-in-Chief in India and was a prominent military figure during the Wellington ministry.'

memes.label: Lord Conyngham
memes.description: 'Henry Conyngham, 1st Marquess Conyngham (1766–1832), an Anglo-Irish courtier who served as Lord Chamberlain to George IV and William IV.'

memes.label: Lord Dalhousie
memes.description: 'George Ramsay, 9th Earl of Dalhousie (1770–1838), Commander-in-Chief in India during the Wellington administration, whose performance and health were subjects of Cabinet discussion.'

memes.label: Lord Darnley
memes.description: 'John Bligh, 4th Earl of Darnley (1767–1831), Irish peer who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as an active participant in House of Lords debates and political discussions.'

memes.label: Lord Dudley
memes.description: 'John Ward, 1st Earl of Dudley (1781–1833), served as Foreign Secretary under Lord Liverpool, Canning, Goderich, and Wellington, and appears frequently in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Lord Durham
memes.description: 'John George Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham (1792–1840), radical Whig politician, colonial reformer, and author of the Durham Report on British North America.'

memes.label: Lord Eldon
memes.description: 'John Scott, 1st Earl of Eldon (1751–1838), Lord Chancellor of Great Britain and an ultra-Tory opponent of Catholic emancipation and parliamentary reform.'

memes.label: Lord Ellenborough
memes.description: 'Edward Law, 1st Earl of Ellenborough (1790–1871), Lord Privy Seal in the Wellington administration and author of this political diary, recording Cabinet deliberations from 1828 to 1830.'

memes.label: Lord Errol
memes.description: 'William Hay, 18th Earl of Erroll (1801–1846) — Scottish peer who served as Master of the Horse to Queen Adelaide, appearing in court and Privy Council contexts.'

memes.label: Lord Falmouth
memes.description: 'Edward Boscawen, 1st Earl of Falmouth (1787–1841), an Ultra-Tory peer and one of the most intransigent opponents of Catholic emancipation in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Lord Farnborough
memes.description: 'Charles Long, 1st Baron Farnborough (1760–1838), a British Tory politician and courtier who served as a close adviser to George IV.'

memes.label: Lord Farnham
memes.description: 'John Maxwell, 2nd Earl of Farnham (1760–1823; or his successor), Irish representative peer and Tory politician active in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Lord Francis Leveson Gower
memes.description: 'Lord Francis Leveson Gower (1800–1857), British Tory politician, Secretary to the Treasury and Chief Secretary for Ireland, appearing frequently in Ellenborough''s political diary of 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Lord Graham
memes.description: "James Graham, styled Lord Graham (1799–1836), Tory politician and later First Commissioner of Woods and Forests in Wellington's administration."

memes.label: Lord Grey
memes.description: 'Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey (1764–1845), Whig leader and future Prime Minister (1830–1834), a prominent opposition figure throughout the diary.'

memes.label: Lord Harrington
memes.description: 'Charles Stanhope, 3rd Earl of Harrington (1753–1829), British general and courtier, Gold Stick of the King''s Household, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary in an episode concerning the Duke of Cumberland and court protocol.'

memes.label: Lord Harrowby
memes.description: 'Dudley Ryder, 1st Earl of Harrowby (1762–1847), a British Tory politician who served in the House of Lords and spoke on the Regency question.'

memes.label: Lord Hertford
memes.description: 'Francis Seymour-Conway, 3rd Marquess of Hertford (1777–1842), British Tory politician and courtier, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a correspondent on matters of foreign policy and patronage.'

memes.label: Lord Heytesbury
memes.description: 'William à Court, 1st Baron Heytesbury (1779–1860), British ambassador to Russia during the Wellington administration and later Viceroy of Ireland.'

memes.label: Lord Hill
memes.description: 'Rowland Hill, 1st Viscount Hill (1772–1842), Commander-in-Chief of the British Army during the Wellington ministry and a distinguished Peninsular War veteran.'

memes.label: Lord Holland
memes.description: 'Henry Vassall-Fox, 3rd Baron Holland (1773–1840), Whig statesman, diarist, and leading figure in Whig society during the late Georgian era.'

memes.label: Lord Jersey
memes.description: 'George Child Villiers, 5th Earl of Jersey (1773–1859), Tory peer and Lord Chamberlain to George IV.'

memes.label: Lord John Russell
memes.description: 'Lord John Russell (1792–1878), leading Whig reformer, architect of the Reform Bill, and twice Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.'

memes.label: Lord Lansdowne
memes.description: 'Henry Petty-Fitzmaurice, 3rd Marquess of Lansdowne (1780–1863), a leading Whig statesman who served in the Grenville and Grey ministries and was a prominent advocate of reform.'

memes.label: Lord Lauderdale
memes.description: 'James Maitland, 8th Earl of Lauderdale (1759–1839), a Scottish Whig peer and political economist who served as a proxy in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Lord Londonderry
memes.description: 'Charles Vane, 3rd Marquess of Londonderry (1778–1854), soldier, politician, and half-brother of Viscount Castlereagh, known in the diary for erratic behaviour in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Lord Lyndhurst
memes.description: 'John Singleton Copley, 1st Baron Lyndhurst (1772–1863), a British lawyer and Tory politician who served three times as Lord Chancellor.'

memes.label: Lord Malmesbury
memes.description: 'James Harris, 2nd Earl of Malmesbury (1778–1841), a Tory peer who supported the Wellington ministry on key divisions and participated actively in the Franchise Bill debates of 1828–1829.'

memes.label: Lord Mansfield
memes.description: 'David William Murray, 3rd Earl of Mansfield (1777–1840), Tory peer and ultra-Protestant activist who organised opposition to the Wellington government after Catholic emancipation.'

memes.label: Lord Maryborough
memes.description: 'William Wellesley-Pole, 3rd Earl of Mornington and 1st Baron Maryborough (1763–1845), Anglo-Irish politician, elder brother of the Duke of Wellington, and former Chief Secretary for Ireland.'

memes.label: Lord Melbourne
memes.description: 'William Lamb, 2nd Viscount Melbourne (1779–1848), Whig politician and Home Secretary (1830–1834), later Prime Minister; in 1828–1830 he appears as a Canningite-Whig figure in opposition to the Wellington government.'

memes.label: Lord Melville
memes.description: 'Robert Dundas, 2nd Viscount Melville (1771–1851), First Lord of the Admiralty in the Wellington administration and a long-serving Tory office-holder.'

memes.label: Lord Morpeth
memes.description: 'George Howard, Viscount Morpeth (1802–1864), later 7th Earl of Carlisle — Whig MP for Yorkshire and a noted parliamentary reformer.'

memes.label: Lord Palmerston
memes.description: 'Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston (1784–1865), Canningite politician who served as Foreign Secretary and later Prime Minister; during 1828–1830 he was a leading opposition figure courted by the Wellington government.'

memes.label: Lord Plunket
memes.description: 'William Plunket, 1st Baron Plunket (1764–1854), Irish lawyer and politician, Lord Chancellor of Ireland, whose powerful speech on Catholic emancipation is recorded in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Lord Radnor
memes.description: 'William Pleydell-Bouverie, 3rd Earl of Radnor (1779–1869), Whig peer and reform advocate in the House of Lords, known for his independent political stance.'

memes.label: Lord Ravensworth
memes.description: 'Thomas Henry Liddell, 1st Baron Ravensworth (1775–1855), British peer and Tory politician who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a confidant of the King on political matters.'

memes.label: Lord Redesdale
memes.description: 'John Freeman-Mitford, 1st Baron Redesdale (1748–1830), British lawyer and politician, former Speaker of the House of Commons and Lord Chancellor of Ireland, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary opposing Chancery reform.'

memes.label: Lord Rosslyn
memes.description: 'James St Clair-Erskine, 2nd Earl of Rosslyn (1762–1837), Lord Privy Seal in the Wellington administration and a key Tory cabinet minister.'

memes.label: Lord Shaftesbury
memes.description: 'Cropley Ashley-Cooper, 6th Earl of Shaftesbury (1768–1851), Tory politician who served as Deputy Speaker of the House of Lords and chaired committees during the Wellington administration.'

memes.label: Lord Stanhope
memes.description: 'Philip Henry Stanhope, 4th Earl Stanhope (1781–1855), British peer and politician who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as an active mover of parliamentary motions on shipping and a persistent questioner of government policy.'

memes.label: Lord Strangford
memes.description: 'Percy Smythe, 6th Viscount Strangford (1780–1855), British diplomat and peer, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a court attendee and source of news from India.'

memes.label: Lord Stuart de Rothesay
memes.description: 'Charles Stuart, 1st Baron Stuart de Rothesay (1779–1845), British Ambassador to France during the Wellington ministry and a key diplomatic channel between London and Paris in the post-Napoleonic period.'

memes.label: Lord Tenterden
memes.description: "Charles Abbott, 1st Baron Tenterden (1762–1832), Lord Chief Justice of the King's Bench, a Tory peer active in the House of Lords."

memes.label: Lord Tweddale
memes.description: 'George Hay, 8th Marquess of Tweeddale (1787–1876), Scottish peer and military officer, briefly mentioned in Ellenborough''s diary as a candidate for an Irish appointment.'

memes.label: Lord Wellesley
memes.description: 'Richard Wellesley, 1st Marquess Wellesley (1760–1842), elder brother of the Duke of Wellington, former Governor-General of India and Foreign Secretary, and a difficult senior figure in the administration.'

memes.label: Lord Westmoreland
memes.description: 'John Fane, 10th Earl of Westmoreland (1759–1841), British Tory politician who served as Lord Privy Seal and appears in Ellenborough''s diary as an active peer in parliamentary and court affairs.'

memes.label: Lord Wharncliffe
memes.description: 'James Archibald Stuart-Wortley-Mackenzie, 1st Baron Wharncliffe (1776–1845), British peer and politician who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as an independent-minded participant in parliamentary debates of 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Lord William Bentinck
memes.description: 'Lord William Cavendish-Bentinck (1774–1839), Governor-General of India from 1828 to 1835, known for social and administrative reforms.'

memes.label: Lord Winchelsea
memes.description: 'George Finch-Hatton, 10th Earl of Winchilsea (1791–1858), an Ultra-Tory peer known for his opposition to Catholic emancipation.'

memes.label: Lord Wynford
memes.description: 'William Draper Best, 1st Baron Wynford (1767–1845), British judge and Tory politician, Chief Justice of the Common Pleas, and a conservative voice on judicial matters in the House of Lords.'

memes.label: Mr. Cartwright
memes.description: 'Mr. Cartwright, a British consul who served at Constantinople and later in Brussels during the Belgian Revolution of 1830.'

memes.label: Mr. Chaplin
memes.description: 'Mr. Chaplin, a British colonial official and expert on Indian affairs who gave evidence before a Parliamentary committee in 1828.'

memes.label: Mr. Elphinstone
memes.description: 'Mountstuart Elphinstone (1779–1859), Governor of Bombay and East India Company administrator, frequently consulted by Lord Ellenborough on Indian affairs.'

memes.label: Mr. Fergusson
memes.description: 'Mr. Fergusson, a legal adviser or official connected with East India Company affairs, appearing in Ellenborough''s diary in matters of colonial legal policy and parliamentary debates.'

memes.label: Mr. Ricketts
memes.description: 'Mr. Ricketts, a witness before a parliamentary committee in March 1830, described in the diary as knowing little about the law and contrasting poorly with another witness.'

memes.label: Mrs. Arbuthnot
memes.description: 'Harriet Arbuthnot (1793–1834), née Fane, close confidante of the Duke of Wellington and diarist, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a key figure in Wellington''s social and political circle.'

memes.label: Mrs. Fox
memes.description: 'Mrs. Fox, a society lady who appears in Ellenborough''s diary at the opening of Parliament and other social occasions in 1829.'

memes.label: Mrs. Hastings
memes.description: 'Mrs. Hastings — widow of a senior East India Company figure, whose pension case was pursued by Ellenborough through Treasury channels as recorded in the diary.'

memes.label: "Parliamentary Reform"
memes.description: "The movement for reforming the British parliamentary system in the late 1820s, a major political issue preceding the Reform Act 1832."

memes.label: Portuguese Civil War
memes.description: 'The Portuguese Civil War (1828–1834) — the dynastic conflict between the absolutist Dom Miguel and his liberal brother Dom Pedro over the Portuguese throne — intersected repeatedly with British diplomacy during the Wellington government, as recorded in Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Prince Charles of Bavaria
memes.description: 'Prince Charles of Bavaria (1795–1875), a Bavarian prince considered as a candidate for the Greek throne during the diplomatic negotiations chronicled in the diary.'

memes.label: Prince Emilius of Hesse
memes.description: 'Prince Emilius of Hesse-Darmstadt, a German prince considered as a candidate for the Greek throne who had previously served as an aide-de-camp to Napoleon Bonaparte.'

memes.label: Prince Frederick of Orange
memes.description: 'Prince Frederick of the Netherlands (1797–1881), second son of King William I of the Netherlands, military commander during the Belgian Revolution.'

memes.label: Prince Frederick of Orange
memes.description: 'Prince Frederick of Orange (1797–1881), second son of William I of the Netherlands, commanded Dutch forces during the Belgian Revolution of 1830.'

memes.label: Prince John of Saxony
memes.description: 'Prince John of Saxony (1801–1873) — Saxon prince and later King of Saxony (1854–1873), who was a candidate for the Greek throne during the period of Ellenborough''s diary.'

memes.label: Prince Leopold of Saxe-Coburg
memes.description: 'Prince Leopold of Saxe-Coburg (1790–1865), later Leopold I, King of the Belgians; a central figure in the Greek succession crisis of 1830.'

memes.label: Prince Philip of Hesse-Homburg
memes.description: 'Prince Philip of Hesse-Homburg (1779–1846), a German prince proposed as a candidate for a newly created European throne in 1828–1830.'

memes.label: Prince Polignac
memes.description: 'Jules de Polignac (1780–1847), ultra-royalist Prime Minister of France from August 1829 to July 1830, whose government was a focus of British diplomatic concern.'

memes.label: Princess Victoria
memes.description: 'Princess Victoria (1819–1901), later Queen Victoria, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary during debates on the Regency Bill and her education in 1830.'

memes.label: Robert Gordon
memes.description: 'Sir Robert Gordon (1791–1847), British diplomat and ambassador to the Ottoman Empire, who managed Anglo-Turkish relations during the Russo-Turkish War.'

memes.label: Robert Peel
memes.description: 'Sir Robert Peel, 2nd Baronet (1788–1850), Home Secretary in Wellington''s government 1828–1830, a key figure in Catholic Emancipation and the diary''s political narrative.'

memes.label: Sir Archibald Campbell
memes.description: 'Sir Archibald Campbell (1769–1843), British Army officer, Governor of British Burma (1826–1828) and later Lieutenant Governor of New Brunswick, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary in discussions of Burmese territorial questions and Indian military policy.'

memes.label: Sir Brook Taylor
memes.description: 'Sir Brook Taylor, British diplomat and minister to Prussia, who appears in Ellenborough''s diary as a channel of diplomatic correspondence between the British government and the King of Prussia.'

memes.label: Sir George Murray
memes.description: 'British Army officer and Tory politician who served as Secretary of State for War and the Colonies from 1828 to 1830.'

memes.label: Sir John Malcolm
memes.description: 'Sir John Malcolm (1769–1833), British soldier, diplomat, and Governor of Bombay, a key figure in British Indian administration.'

memes.label: Sir Stratford Canning
memes.description: 'Stratford Canning (1786–1880), British diplomat, ambassador to Constantinople, and cousin of George Canning, involved in the Greek question during the period of the diary.'

memes.label: Sir Sidney Beckwith
memes.description: 'Sir Sidney Beckwith, a British military officer appointed to the command at Bombay and mentioned in Ellenborough''s diary among senior military figures at official gatherings.'

memes.label: Sir Thomas Munro
memes.description: 'Sir Thomas Munro, 1st Baronet (1761–1827), a Scottish soldier and colonial administrator who served as Governor of Madras and wrote influential memoranda on Indian governance.'

memes.label: Slavery Abolition
memes.description: 'The movement to abolish slavery and suppress the transatlantic slave trade was a persistent issue in British politics during 1828–1830, intersecting with diplomacy, colonial governance, and parliamentary reform, as documented in Ellenborough''s political diary.'

memes.label: Tory Party
memes.description: 'The Tory Party, the dominant British political party in the 1820s, led by the Duke of Wellington and Robert Peel, whose internal divisions over Catholic Emancipation and reform defined the diary''s political narrative.'

memes.label: Whig Party
memes.description: 'The Whig Party — the principal opposition to the Duke of Wellington''s Tory government — underwent a transformation during 1828–1830, moving from division and weakness towards the coalition that would pass the Great Reform Act and dominate British politics for the next generation, as traced in Ellenborough''s political diary.'

memes.label: William I of the Netherlands
memes.description: 'William I (1772–1843), King of the Netherlands and Grand Duke of Luxembourg, whose reign was shaken by the Belgian Revolution of 1830.'
